title: Chicken shawarma
subtitle: Yoghurt-marinated chicken thighs roasted with warm spices
excerpt: Chicken thighs marinated overnight in yoghurt, garlic, and a blend of shawarma spices (cumin, turmeric, allspice, cinnamon, paprika), then roasted in a hot oven until the edges catch and char. Served in pitta with garlic sauce, tomato, and pickles. The oven version lacks the rotisserie spit but the marinade does most of the work.
sourceNotes: Shawarma is a Levantine street food descended from the Ottoman doner kebab tradition, with the rotating vertical spit documented in Ottoman cooking from the nineteenth century. The Lebanese, Syrian, and Palestinian street version uses chicken or lamb and is typically served in flatbread with garlic sauce or tahini. This oven-roasted version is the standard home adaptation documented in Anissa Helou's 'Feast' (2018) and Yotam Ottolenghi's cookbooks.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate overnight for best results. The cooked chicken can be sliced and kept warm in a low oven for 20 minutes.

content.text: The shawarma spice blend is what matters here. Allspice, cinnamon, and turmeric are the notes that distinguish shawarma from other marinated chicken dishes — warm, slightly sweet, aromatic rather than hot. The yoghurt in the marinade acts as a tenderiser and helps the spices penetrate the meat. Chicken thighs are the right cut: they have enough fat to withstand a high oven and produce the slightly charred edges that approximate the rotisserie crust.

The oven needs to be very hot to produce the right result. At 220°C, the top surface of the chicken should be charring at the edges by 25 to 30 minutes. If the oven is not hot enough, the chicken will steam rather than roast and the skin will not colour properly. Resting for 5 minutes before slicing keeps the meat moist.

Mix the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{garlic}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{cumin-ground}}, {{allspice}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{cayenne}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and salt together in a large bowl. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} and turn to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the chicken pieces skin-side up on a roasting pan, leaving space between them. Roast for 25 to 30 minutes until the skin is deeply coloured and charring at the edges and the juices run clear. Rest for 5 minutes before slicing.
Slice the chicken off the bone in thin pieces. Warm the {{pitta-bread}} briefly in the oven or a dry pan. Pile the chicken into the pitta with garlic sauce or tahini, tomato, cucumber, and pickles.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale and soft rather than charred
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or chicken pieces too crowded
items.fix: The oven needs to be at full fan temperature with the pan near the top. Leave space between pieces so they roast rather than steam. Finish under the grill for 3 to 4 minutes if needed

items.cause: Overcooked or boneless breasts used instead of thighs
items.fix: Use bone-in thighs — they are more forgiving of high heat. Check the internal temperature at 25 minutes: 74°C is the target; pull immediately and rest
The allspice and turmeric need time to penetrate; a 4-hour minimum and overnight ideally. Short-marinated chicken just tastes of yoghurt

items.cause: Normal reaction — the sugars in the yoghurt will char
items.fix: This is correct: the charred bits on the pan carry a lot of flavour. Scrape them up and add them to the sliced chicken when serving

content.text: Shawarma descends from the Ottoman döner kebab tradition — the vertical rotating spit that produces the thin-shaved meat sold in Turkish and Arab restaurants across the world. The word 'shawarma' likely derives from the Turkish 'çevirme' (turning). In Lebanon and Syria, shawarma (particularly the chicken version) is the default street food: late-night, cheap, eaten from paper with garlic sauce running down the forearm. The oven version loses the rotation and the spit drip but keeps everything else, and in a domestic kitchen it is the most practical way to approximate the flavour of the original.
